findings.location: Success Criteria: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: The success criterion 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' is not in-session evaluable — it requires a comparative measurement (agent revision outcomes) that is post-sprint telemetry, not a repo artifact or deterministic command. There is no stated metric, target, or gold set against which 'measurably help' can be checked at close. Several other criteria are observable (claim blocks without signature; sidecar event emitted), but this one is effort/outcome-vague as written. Note the experiment-grounding AC is already marked [x]/done.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword to an in-session-checkable form, e.g. 'Layer-2 emits coaching findings conforming to the coaching spec (grounded, specific, severity-ranked, checklist-form), verified by unit tests over fixture plans' and move 'measurably helps revision' to the F1 follow-on (offline FP/remediation tooling).

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list vs Success Criteria; no explicit proving commands/steps section
findings.finding: The plan is structured as a list of deliverable ACs rather than executable steps with proving commands, so check (a) 'every completion-relevant claim has a concrete proving command' and (d) 'union of steps reaches the end-state' cannot be confirmed from the text. Most ACs are observable end-states (claim blocks, sidecar event emitted, config knobs exist) and would map to tests, but no verification commands or test plan are named, and one success criterion ('measurably help an agent revise the plan') has no proving command. This needs author input on the intended verification approach rather than asserting it's wrong.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief verification line per AC (e.g. 'verified by integration test: claim with no attestation exits non-zero; with valid signature succeeds') and a proving check for the coaching-quality criterion.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate'; Context 'This epic builds...'; Acceptance Criteria (12+ items spanning Layer-1, Layer-2, registry, attestation, claim integration, chunking/routing, feedback, sidecar event, config, docs)
findings.finding: This is explicitly framed as an EPIC ('This epic builds...') with ~12 acceptance criteria and 6 success criteria that join multiple independent goals via structural 'and' (deterministic Layer-1 floor, advisory Layer-2 reviewer, criteria registry/orchestrator, attestation signing, claim integration, chunking/routing/budget engine, sidecar observability, config surface, docs). It fails the single-concern test and is far above the >6-success-criteria threshold and the >3-files/≥3-layers sizing signals, yet no child decomposition is present in the plan itself. An epic-scale body with no children is a MAJOR decomposition gap; this cannot be executed coherently in one session.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into linked child tickets (e.g. Layer-1 floor; criteria registry + orchestrator; Layer-2 advisory reviewer; attestation/signing + claim integration; chunking/routing/budget; REVIEW_RESULT sidecar; config surface; docs) and keep this as the parent epic, or state explicitly that children will be filed and reference them.

findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3: "Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan"; AC "Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec"
findings.finding: The success criterion 'coaching findings that MEASURABLY help an agent revise the plan' states no in-session observable outcome and no proving mechanism. 'Measurably help' implies a comparative effectiveness study (remediation-rate uplift) that is post-sprint telemetry, not evaluable in-session against a repo artifact or the closing PR's CI. As written it is outcome-vague/effort-framed at the value layer. By contrast the Layer-1 and attestation/claim/sidecar criteria are crisp and in-session evaluable.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword to an in-session observable: e.g. 'Layer-2 emits findings that each carry {criterion id, file/section location, severity, suggested-fix-when-confident, checklist line}, verified by a unit/fixture test asserting the finding schema on sample plans.' Move 'measurably help / remediation uplift' to the F1 follow-on (offline analysis), which the plan already scopes out.

findings.location: AC "escalation by size+complexity+centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)" and "Experiment-grounded defaults" knobs; plus "AGENT ... criteria ... gated by proportionate scrutiny"
findings.finding: Most scope decisions are concretely pinned (tiers, base_chunk values, size_factor, budget formula, fail-open semantics), so the plan is largely executable without clarification. Two items are underspecified design choices rather than placeholders: (1) how 'centrality/blast-radius' is computed at plan time, and (2) the exact 'proportionate scrutiny' gating function that decides which AGENT criteria fire. These are not flagged as TBD but a leaf executor would need the formula. Whether this blocks depends on whether the cited session log / DSO specs define them — which I cannot inspect.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a one-line pointer per item to where the centrality computation and the proportionate-scrutiny gating thresholds are specified (DSO spec section or experiment doc), or carve them into a named sub-ticket if not yet decided.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (all bullets are unchecked outcome statements with no proving command); Success Criteria bullet 3 "measurably help"
findings.finding: Most ACs describe end-states but none names a proving command/check that would produce evidence on success (e.g. how is 'fails open on any unsupported language/tool' demonstrated — a fixture with an unsupported lang asserting skipped=pass?). The 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' claim has no in-session proving step at all (gap between 'what done looks like' and any described step). The plan also lacks an explicit verification section mapping each criterion to its proof, so coverage of the union-reaches-end-state check cannot be confirmed.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Verification' subsection mapping each AC to a concrete check (unit test / fixture / CLI invocation), especially: a fail-open fixture per unsupported-stack path, a claim-block test for missing/stale signature with --force, a material-edit invalidation test, and a REVIEW_RESULT-emitted-and-reducer-ignored assertion.

findings.location: Context section: "when an agent claims a ticket ... nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound" and "COACH agents to author better plans"; Design summary 'rebar FEATURE used by a BROAD, DIVERSE CLIENT BASE'
Title "Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)" vs body Design summary 'fires at open->in_progress', 'inverse of the completion-verifier'; bugs explicitly scoped out

Document self-describes as 'this epic' / 'This epic builds a plan-review verification gate', yet the AC list is a single flat block of ~12 large deliverables with no children; Success Criteria has 6 bullets and AC fails single-concern (Layer-1 floor AND Layer-2 advisory AND registry AND attestation AND claim integration AND chunking/routing AND sidecar/observability AND docs).
findings.finding: The ticket explicitly calls itself an epic and joins many structurally independent goals with 'and' (deterministic floor, advisory LLM review, criteria registry, signing/attestation, claim integration, budget/chunking, observability sidecar, docs). By the criterion's epic test (structural 'and' of independent goals, mixes subsystems, >6 success criteria) it should have children, but no decomposition into child tickets is present. The 'Follow-on work' list explicitly defers OTHER tickets, confirming the author thinks in tickets — yet the core epic itself is not split into executable leaves.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into child tickets along the natural seams (e.g. L1-floor, L2-advisory-criteria, registry+orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, chunking/routing/budget, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, docs) and convert this ticket into a true parent whose ACs are 'children X..N landed & integrated', so each leaf is one-session executable.

findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — the AC list contains no testing/TDD work items; e.g. AC 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open...' and 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force...' name deliverables but no corresponding tests. Compare to Success Criterion 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'.
findings.finding: The plan describes a substantial behavioral surface (fail-open on unsupported stacks, material-edit invalidation, --force override, cap-hit INDETERMINATE shedding, reducer-ignored sidecar) but no acceptance criterion names testing work for any of these. Several of these are exactly failure/boundary paths the criterion requires to be tested: fail-open on tool crash/timeout/unsupported-lang (skipped=PASS), stale/invalidated signature blocking claim, INDETERMINATE on budget cap-hit, and the reducer skipping the REVIEW_RESULT type. As written the work is not testable-by-construction; the fail-open path in particular ('crash/timeout → skipped = PASS, never FAIL') is a critical safety behavior with no named failure-path test.
findings.suggested_edit: Add explicit test ACs (or a 'Testing' section) naming at least: (1) fail-open behavior — for each tool (solidlsp/ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard) a test that an unsupported-lang/no-server/crash/timeout yields skipped=PASS and coverage is recorded; (2) claim gate — stale/material-edit-invalidated signature blocks, valid signature passes, --force override is recorded; (3) budget cap-hit yields INDETERMINATE and sheds AGENT/overlay first; (4) reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT (asserts head-state unchanged). For each, state the observable outcome (not 'works correctly').

findings.location: ## Design summary 'claim is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation; the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND' and Acceptance Criteria 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'.
findings.finding: This plan adds substantial LLM/compute paths (AGENT criteria ~$0.12 + ~50s each; heavy epic ~minutes per Experiment-grounded defaults). The hot-path operation (claim) is explicitly designed to be fast and out-of-band, and resource_efficiency is well addressed (budget cap, chunk-atomic checkpointing, prompt caching, exec-tier shedding). However, the fast claim check itself has no time-bounded done-definition — 'FAST' is asserted but no latency target/budget is given for the on-claim signature-validation path. Minor because the heavy work is correctly off the hot path; only the claim-check latency bound is unstated.
findings.suggested_edit: State a concrete latency target/budget for the on-claim attestation check (e.g. 'claim signature validation completes in <X ms, no network/LLM call on the claim path') so 'FAST' is verifiable.

findings.location: ## Design summary 'Attestation: signed result ... signed certification' and 'the signature stays a normal head-state event'; Acceptance Criteria 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding ... (extends the close-gate signing)'.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a signed cryptographic attestation/certification consumed as a CI rigor signal, plus a --force override and recorded override justifications — these touch trust boundaries (signature forgery/replay, override authorization, secret/key handling for signing). The plan does not state how signing keys are managed or how the signature resists forgery/replay/staleness-bypass, but it explicitly reuses the existing close-gate signing mechanism ('extends the close-gate signing'), so the key-handling and signature-integrity model may be inherited and out of scope for this plan. Cannot decide without inspecting the close-gate signing implementation.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a one-line note clarifying the signature integrity/trust model is inherited from the close-gate signing (or, if new key material is introduced, name how keys are managed and how forgery/replay/stale-signature reuse is prevented).

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last item: 'Docs: how the gate works, the config surface, fail-open behavior, the cert semantics, and the CI-check pattern.' and Design summary 'Routing pivots on has_children + complexity'.
findings.finding: This plan introduces a new pipeline stage (the plan-review gate), new cross-component contracts (criteria registry descriptors, reuse of rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, a new REVIEW_RESULT event type), and many evolving thresholds (base_chunk, size_factor, budget cap, escalation by size+complexity+centrality). Coupling_risk and changeability are well handled: coupling is acknowledged via the declarative registry/extension-point reuse, and thresholds are explicitly config-overridable. The documentation sub-check is partially met (a docs AC exists and a design session log + experiments doc are cited), but there is no named ADR / CLAUDE.md update capturing the novel architectural decision (the inverse-of-completion-verifier gate, the exec-tier-as-primary-axis routing). Minor since user-facing docs are required.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the Docs AC an ADR (or CLAUDE.md/design-doc) entry recording the core architectural decisions — the open→in_progress gate as inverse of the close gate, exec-tier-as-primary-axis chunking, and the routing-on-(has_children+complexity)-not-type choice — so the rationale is captured beyond the session log.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (all 12 items) and Success Criteria — e.g. 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open' and 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented'
findings.finding: The plan is testable-by-claim but not testable-by-construction: no acceptance criterion names a verification method or an observable test for any deliverable. The fail-open behavior (the stated 'IRONCLAD' guarantee: 'Unsupported lang / no server / crash / timeout → skipped = PASS, never FAIL') is exactly the failure/timeout/invalid path that must be tested, yet no AC names a test that injects a tool crash/timeout/unsupported-language and asserts skipped=PASS. Similarly, material-edit invalidation, the budget-cap → INDETERMINATE shedding path, and chunk-atomic checkpointing/resume are stateful/failure behaviors with no described test. AC for these reads as 'implemented' / 'works' rather than an observable assertion.
findings.suggested_edit: Add explicit test ACs: e.g. 'Fail-open test: given an unsupported language and given a forced LSP/ast-grep/syft crash and timeout, Layer-1 records skipped=PASS (not FAIL) and coverage reflects the skip'; 'Invalidation test: a description/AC/file_impact/decomposition edit invalidates the signature and blocks claim, while tag/comment/link/assignee edits do not'; 'Budget-cap test: on cap-hit the review emits INDETERMINATE and sheds AGENT/overlay criteria first, never the DET base'; 'Checkpoint test: an interrupted review resumes at chunk granularity without re-running completed chunks.'

findings.location: Design summary 'Execution' and Experiment-grounded defaults — 'AGENT (tool-using) criteria run ONE agent each (≈85× a single-turn call)' and the claim claim '`claim` is a FAST check for a valid signed attestation; the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND'
findings.finding: The plan adds LLM/compute calls and per-item LLM patterns, so this overlay applies. Two gaps: (1) The claim-time check is described as 'FAST' but has no time-bounded done-definition — no latency target/SLA is stated for the synchronous claim path that gates open→in_progress. (2) The per-criterion AGENT pattern (one agent loop per AGENT criterion, '≈85×' and '~50s each') is precisely an O(n) LLM-calls-per-item shape across criteria/overlays; the plan bounds COST (budget cap) and notes prompt caching but never states a latency/wall-clock target for the out-of-band review (only 'heavy epic ~$1.5–2.0 + minutes'). Latency is bounded only implicitly via budget.
findings.suggested_edit: State an explicit latency done-definition for the synchronous `claim` attestation check (e.g. signature-verify p95 < Xms, no network round-trip), and a wall-clock bound or expected-duration target for the out-of-band review tiers, parallel to the existing per-plan budget cap.

findings.location: Design summary 'Execution' / 'Attestation' and AC 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event' — e.g. 'chunk-atomic checkpointing; INDETERMINATE on cap-hit sheds AGENT/overlay criteria first' and 'the signature stays a normal head-state event'
findings.finding: The plan adds external integration (LLM/tool calls), file I/O, and write operations (sidecar REVIEW_RESULT event + signature head-state event), so reliability applies. error_handling is partially covered via fail-open (skipped=PASS) and INDETERMINATE on cap-hit, which is good. However failover/idempotency is under-specified for the writes: the plan does not state that the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + signature event writes are idempotent/atomic together, nor what happens if the review completes but the signature/sidecar write fails partway (partial state). 'chunk-atomic checkpointing' covers resume of the review compute but not the durability/atomicity of the two emitted events.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a reliability note/AC: the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar and the signature head-state event are written idempotently (re-running a completed review does not duplicate events; a partial write leaves the ticket in a safe, claim-blocking state rather than a falsely-signed one).

findings.location: Design summary 'Attestation' and Success Criteria 'The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process' — 'signed certification', 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a cryptographic signing/attestation boundary (a trust signal consumed by external CI), which is a security-relevant addition. The data_protection sub-check is unaddressed: the plan does not state where the signing key lives, how it is protected, or what prevents a client from forging/self-issuing a 'process followed' signature (the cert is explicitly a CI rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal, so its integrity is the whole point). access_classification proper (endpoints/auth) is largely not-applicable. The override path is logged ('override logging', '--force+justification'), which is good. The gap is key/trust-model specification for the signature.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief security note on the attestation trust model: signing-key custody, what binds the signature to a genuine review run (vs a forged/self-issued one), and how the external CI verifier establishes trust — even if 'inherits the close-gate signing scheme' is the answer, state it explicitly.

findings.location: Design summary → 'Trigger / enforcement: ... the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND'; AC 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The single most surprising gap for the implementer: the plan asserts the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND and that claim only does a fast signature check, but it never specifies the INVOCATION SURFACE that actually runs Layer-1/Layer-2 and produces the signature. There is no named command/entrypoint (e.g. `rebar review <ticket>`), no description of where it runs (local CLI? CI? agent-triggered?), and no statement of how an agent that just got blocked at claim is supposed to TRIGGER the review and obtain a fresh signature. Without this, the whole gate is unrunnable as scoped: every AC depends on a review having executed, but the act of executing the review is undefined. Second concern: the attestation is 'bound to the latest MATERIAL event commit' and 'material-edit invalidates', but the plan gives no concrete, deterministic definition of how the material-vs-non-material boundary is COMPUTED at claim time across the diverse client base — only an example list (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition vs tags/comments/links/assignee). The classification rule is the load-bearing mechanism for staleness and is left implicit, which will cause rework and inconsistent invalidation. Third concern: 'fail-open IRONCLAD' is stated as a posture but the failure-mode interaction with the BLOCKING Layer-1 floor is not reconciled — if a Layer-1 tool (e.g. solidlsp) crashes mid-review, the plan says skipped=PASS, but it does not say how coverage degradation is signaled to the claim gate or to CI such that a near-empty fail-open review can't masquerade as a strong certification; the 'coverage recorded on the attestation' line names the field but not the threshold/semantics that prevent a vacuous pass from being treated as rigor.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit 'Invocation & lifecycle' subsection: name the review entrypoint/command, state who triggers it (agent on claim-block vs CI vs manual) and where it runs, and trace the loop claim-block → run review → emit signature → re-claim. Add a precise material-event classification rule (enumerate event types or define the predicate over edited fields) rather than examples. Define the 'coverage' field's semantics and a minimum-coverage signal so CI/claim can distinguish a substantively-passed review from a fail-open-skipped one.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)' section; Criteria registry section; Attestation AC; Context paragraph 1
findings.finding:
The plan makes multiple confident existence/reuse assertions that are either unverified or contradicted by the actual codebase. Specifically:

**[FAIL] Assertion: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'** (Design summary, Polyglot tooling). A full glob of `src/rebar/**` and the entire repo reveals no `solidlsp` file, module, or integration. The phrase 'already in repo' is a direct confident-existence trigger — zero evidence found.

**[FAIL] Assertion: 'ast-grep (resolution/callers)'** (same section). No ast-grep integration, wrapper, or invocation exists anywhere in the source tree. Unverified.

**[FAIL] Assertion: 'syft + registry + LLM-fallback (deps)'** (same section). No syft dependency or integration found in `src/`, `pyproject.toml` (which lists only `pyyaml` as a core dep and no syft in any optional group), or tests. Unverified.

**[FAIL] Assertion: 'scc + lizard (size)'** (same section). No scc or lizard integration found in the source tree or pyproject extras. Unverified.

**[FAIL] Assertion: 'reusing rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' with exec/facet/context_needs/routing/trigger fields** (Criteria registry section and AC 'Criteria registry'). While `catalog.json`, `runner.py`, `prompts.py`, and `findings.py` are confirmed real, `catalog.json` contains NONE of the claimed extension fields (`exec`, `facet`, `context_needs`, `routing`, `trigger`, `block_policy`). The plan treats these as existing structure to reuse, but they do not exist in the actual schema — they are net-new additions. The reuse assertion overstates what is already there.

**[FAIL] Assertion: 'session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b (plan-of-record v4)'** (Context). The session-logs directory exists with 6 files, none matching this ID. The plan-of-record v4 source document cannot be located in the repo — meaning downstream agents cannot verify design intent from the cited source.

**[PASS] Assertion: completion-verifier 'already in repo'** — Confirmed: `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` and a `catalog.json` entry both exist.

**[PASS] Assertion: 'extends the close-gate signing'** — `src/rebar/signing.py` with HMAC-SHA256 + SIGNATURE event exists and is a real foundation to extend.

**[PASS] Assertion: docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-storage-and-chunking.md and dso-criteria-specs.md exist** — Both confirmed present.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. For solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard: change 'already in repo' to 'to be added as new integrations' and add AC items for installing/wrapping each tool. Do not claim they exist.
2. For catalog.json extension: add an explicit AC item — 'Extend catalog.json descriptor schema with exec, facet, context_needs, routing, trigger, block_policy fields (net-new additions, not pre-existing)' — rather than framing it as reuse of existing structure.
3. For the session log reference: either add the session log file to the repo under `session-logs/63cc-ec94-4792-464b.md`, or replace the reference with the existing `docs/experiments/` documents that ARE present and verifiable.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling (fail-open IRONCLAD)' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — Layer-1 deterministic criteria bullet; Experiment-grounded defaults section
findings.finding: Three specific tooling artifacts the plan relies on for Layer-1 deterministic checks — `solidlsp`, `syft`, `ast-grep`, `scc`, and `lizard` — have no corresponding files or modules anywhere in the codebase. Glob searches for `*solidlsp*`, `*syft*`, `*ast_grep*`, `*scc*`, and `*lizard*` all returned zero matches. The plan states these are 'already in repo' (specifically: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'), but no file, directory, wrapper, or import of solidlsp was found. If solidlsp is genuinely absent, the Layer-1 resolution and caller-lookup checks have no implementation anchor — and the plan's claim that they exist becomes a hallucination. The other four tools (syft, ast-grep, scc, lizard) have no stubs, config entries, wrapper scripts, or dependency declarations in sight. For changes of behavior: `signing.py` (the existing close-gate signing the plan extends) is confirmed to exist at `src/rebar/signing.py`, and `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`, `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py`, `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/catalog.json` all exist — the plan's references to 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' are grounded. However, `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/catalog.json` is NOT currently structured as a criteria registry (it lists reviewer dimensions, not per-criterion descriptors carrying exec/facet/context_needs/routing/trigger fields). The plan says a new 'criteria registry' will be added with those fields — it is unclear whether the plan intends to extend this existing catalog or create a new file; the distinction matters for whether existing catalog consumers need updating. Consumer scope gap: `src/rebar/llm/operations.py`, `src/rebar/llm/review_workflows.py`, and the `rebar claim` CLI path (confirmed at `src/rebar/_cli/help/claim.txt` and `tests/interfaces/__pycache__/test_e3_claim.cpython-314-pytest-9.1.0.pyc`) are all behavioral touch-points the plan references (claim integration, reuse of llm extension points). The plan mentions updating `claim` but does not enumerate which other modules in `src/rebar/llm/` or `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/` consume the signing or operations APIs and would need to be aware of the new attestation check. The `REVIEW_RESULT` event type requires the reducer to skip it — no reducer file was found in the glob (reducer logic likely lives in the engine binary or a non-Python layer), and the plan does not name the specific file/symbol to be patched.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Clarify or correct the claim 'solidlsp (already in repo)' — if it is not present, replace with 'solidlsp (to be integrated)' and add an AC item for its integration/installation. 2. Add a file-impact list explicitly enumerating: the new criteria-registry file (path, schema), the new plan-review gate module (path), extensions to signing.py (new functions), the claim CLI module (file path + function), and the reducer file where REVIEW_RESULT must be registered as a skip type. 3. For the catalog.json reuse, state explicitly whether the existing file is extended (requiring consumer update audits) or a new parallel file is created. 4. Name any reconciler or engine modules that call signing or the claim path and confirm whether they need changes.

findings.location: Design summary — 'Two layers', 'Criteria registry', 'Attestation', 'claim integration'
